coredata.dc:title: Anchoring nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on nickel carbonate hydroxide nanosheets for hybrid supercapacitor applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Zhenyuan
Ma, Dongwei
Dai, Wenyao
Liu, Kai
Shen, Xiaoping
Zhu, Guoxing
Nie, Yunjin
Pasang, Drolma
Yuan, Aihua
coredata.dc:description: With the ever-increasing demands for energy resources, the exploration of high energy density hybrid supercapacitors is urgently required. Herein, an effective hydrothermal strategy is demonstrated to construct advanced cathode of nickel carbonate hydroxide (NCH) ultrathin nanosheets anchored nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) for hybrid supercapacitors. It is revealed that the NCQDs are evenly deposited on the surface of NCH nanosheets, which provide abundant active sites for NCH nanosheets and endow them improved electrochemical characteristics. The NCH/NCQDs nanosheets deliver a decent electrochemical capacity of 727C g−1 at 1 A g−1 with ameliorative rate capability and cyclic stability. In addition, a hybrid supercapacitor device with an impressive energy density (49.1 Wh kg−1 at 700.3 W kg−1) and stable cycling property (87.5% after 8000 cycles) is fabricated by employing NCH/NCQDs nanosheets and p-phenylenediamine functionalized reduced graphene oxide (PRGO) as cathode and anode. These results indicate the great potential of NCH/NCQDs nanosheets for renewable energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel carbonate hydroxide
Carbon quantum dots
Nanosheets
Energy density

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) SEM and (c, d) TEM images of the NCH nanosheets.
(a) SEM, (b, c) TEM, (d) HRTEM and (e-i) TEM mapping images of the NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets.

(a) XRD patterns of NCH and NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets; (b) Ni 2p, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s spectra of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets; (e) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (f) pore size distribution of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets.

(a) CV profiles of NCH/NCQDs-2 composites at 0.2–1.0 mV s−1; (b) Plots of log (i) versus log (v); (c) The capacitance contribution of NCH/NCQDs-2 at different sweep rates; (d) GCD curves of NCH/NCQDs-2 at 1–15 A g−1; (e) GCD curves of NCH and various NCH/NCQDs composites at 1 A g−1; (f) Electrochemical capacities of NCH and various NCH/NCQDs composites at 1–15 A g−1.

(a) CV curves of NCH/NCQDs-2 and PRGO at 5 mV s−1; (b) CV curves of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO device at 5–50 mV s−1; (c) GCD curves and (d) specific capacitances of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO device at 1–20 A g−1; (e) Ragone plots of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO; (f) Cyclic property of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO at 10 A g−1 over 8000 cycles, the inset is the first and last five cycles during the cycling test.

head.ce:title: Anchoring nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on nickel carbonate hydroxide nanosheets for hybrid supercapacitor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang 212003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the ever-increasing demands for energy resources, the exploration of high energy density hybrid supercapacitors is urgently required. Herein, an effective hydrothermal strategy is demonstrated to construct advanced cathode of nickel carbonate hydroxide (NCH) ultrathin nanosheets anchored nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) for hybrid supercapacitors. It is revealed that the NCQDs are evenly deposited on the surface of NCH nanosheets, which provide abundant active sites for NCH nanosheets and endow them improved electrochemical characteristics. The NCH/NCQDs nanosheets deliver a decent electrochemical capacity of 727C g−1 at 1 A g−1 with ameliorative rate capability and cyclic stability. In addition, a hybrid supercapacitor device with an impressive energy density (49.1 Wh kg−1 at 700.3 W kg−1) and stable cycling property (87.5% after 8000 cycles) is fabricated by employing NCH/NCQDs nanosheets and p-phenylenediamine functionalized reduced graphene oxide (PRGO) as cathode and anode. These results indicate the great potential of NCH/NCQDs nanosheets for renewable energy storage.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the rapid development of consumer electronics has triggered plentiful efforts to explore advanced electrochemical energy devices [1–3]. As energy storage devices, supercapacitors have been intensively investigated owing to their charming advantages such as high power densities and long lifespan [4–6]. However, the limited energy densities of supercapacitors largely hamper the commercial applications of them [7,8]. Hybrid supercapacitors, constructing with battery-type cathode and capacitive anode have attracted considerable attention on account of their enhanced energy densities compared with conventional supercapacitors [9]. The energy densities of hybrid supercapacitors greatly rely on the electrochemical characteristics of their battery-type cathodes [10]. Therefore, the design appropriate battery-type cathodes with outstanding performance is of great importance for hybrid supercapacitors. Hydroxycarbonates are ubiquity in nature and have captured widespread attention in energy storage [11,12]. The general formula of hydroxycarbonates with divalent metal ions is M2(CO3)(OH)2 (M = Ni, Co, Cu, Zn, etc.) [13,14]. The octahedral chains of M2+ are connected by triangular CO3 2–. This unique crystal structure of M2(CO3)(OH)2 allows electrolyte ions to freely intercalate into the internal area of the materials [15]. Moreover, the hydrophilicity of CO3 2– can effectively improve the wettability and reduce the polarization of M2(CO3)(OH)2, which make the electrode materials more reversible [16,17]. Among these hydroxycarbonates, nickel carbonate hydroxide (NCH) is considered as a promising cathode for hybrid supercapacitors in terms of its environmental friendliness and superior electrochemical capacity [18,19]. Nevertheless, the inferior cycling durabiliand low capacity retention are the key challenges of NCH materials for hybrid supercapacitors due to their poor electron transport and ion deintercalation capabilities.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new family of carbonaceous materials, have drawn considerable attention as a result of their unparalleled quantum effects [20]. CQDs possess exceptional advantages, such as high solubility, large surface area, facile surface functionalization, and superb conductivity, which make them promising alternatives for plentiful exciting applications [21,22]. In particular, the emergence of CQDs has shown great prospects in the fields of energy storage [23]. Whereas, the applications of CQDs in hybrid supercapacitors are still in infancy. In addition, the electrochemical characteristics of CQDs-based materials are closely related to their morphologies [24]. The optimized architecture can supply a shorter transport path for electrolyte ions and improve the diffusion kinetics [25]. Therefore, the construction of CQDs-based materials with specific morphologies is highly desirable.

Herein, a facile hydrothermal strategy for the development of nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) deposited on NCH ultrathin nanosheets is proposed. The presence of NCQDs can reduce the size of NCH nanosheets and remarkably optimize the electrochemical capacities of them. The obtained NCH/NCQDs ultrathin nanosheets deliver an enhanced electrochemical capacity of 727C g−1 at 1 A g−1 with improved rate capability (66.4% at 15 A g−1) and cyclic stability (82.4% after 5000 cycles). Hybrid supercapacitor device based on NCH/NCQDs nanosheets and p-phenylenediamine functionalized reduced graphene oxide (PRGO) exhibits an impressive energy density of 49.1 Wh kg−1 at 700.3 W kg−1 and excellent cycling durability with a capacitance retention of 87.5% after 8000 cycles.

The NCQDs were synthesized according to our previous work [26]. For the preparation of NCH/NCQDs nanosheets, nickel acetate tetrahydrate (4 mmoL), NCQDs powders (0, 20, 30 and 40 mg) and hexamethylenetetramine (250 mg) were added to the mixture of ethanol (15 mL) and ethylene glycol (15 mL) by ultrasonic vibration. The mixed solution was subsequently feeded to a 50 mL of autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained solids were centrifuged, rinsed with distilled water and ethanol thoroughly and subsequently dried at 50 °C. The powders were labeled as NCH, NCH/NCQDs-1, NCH/NCQDs-2 and NCH/NCQDs-3 according to the amounts of NCQDs.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the nanosheets were observed on ZEISS Merlin Compact and JEOL JEM 2100F microscopes. The heights of NCQDs, NCH and NCH/NCQDs were investigated using a Cypher ES atomic force microscope (AFM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of NCH and NCH/NCQDs nanosheets were analyzed on Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was carried out on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha + spectrometer. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of NCH/NCQDs nanosheets were measured on micrometrics ASAP2460 analyzer.

The electrochemical investigation was conducted on a CHI760E electrochemical workstation in 3 M KOH. In half-cell tests, Hg/HgO electrode and Pt plate were used as the reference electrode and the counter electrode. For fabricating the working electrode, the NCH/NCQDs nanosheets (80%), acetylene black (10%) and polytetrafluoroethylene (10%) were thoroughly grinded in ethanol. Subsequently, the obtained slice (approximately 2 mg) was coated on the Ni foam at 10 Mpa and placed in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The electrochemical capacity (C, C g−1) of NCH/NCQDs nanosheets was determined from the Eq. (1) [27]:
(1)
C = I t / m
where I (A), t (s) and m (g) represent the current density, the discharge time and the mass of NCH/NCQDs nanosheets, respectively. In full-cell tests, a hybrid supercapacitor was equipped by NCH/NCQDs cathode and PRGO anode (NCH/NCQDs//PRGO). The fabrication of PRGO anode is the same as NCH/NCQDs electrode [28,29]. The weight ratio of NCH/NCQDs to PRGO was optimized on the base of charge balance [30]. The energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, W kg−1) of NCH/NCQDs//PRGO were calculated according to the following relationships [31]:
(2)
E = I ∫ V d t / ( 3.6 M )
(3)
P = 3600 E / t
where V (V) is the voltage in the discharge process and M (g) is the total weight of NCH/NCQDs and PRGO. The cycling property of NCH/NCQDs//PRGO hybrid supercapacitor was performed on a Neware CT-3008 battery test system.

The morphologies of the NCH and NCH/NCQDs-2 composites were elucidated by SEM and TEM techniques. Fig. 1 a presents a representative low-magnification SEM image of the NCH composites, which possess curved interconnected flake-like structure. Particularly, the NCH nanosheets display a rough surface according to the magnified SEM image in Fig. 1b. The TEM images (Fig. 1c and 1d) also confirm the obvious flake-like architecture. The wrinkled nanosheets with transparent physiognomy lie on the carbon membrane of copper grid, which indicates the ultrathin nature of NCH. The SEM and TEM images of NCH/NCQDs-2 in Fig. 2 a and 2b clearly suggest that the composites also exhibit a flake-like morphology, which is similar to the bare NCH. However, the size of NCH/NCQDs-2 becomes smaller as compared to NCH composites. This phenomenon is consistent with our previous research [32], and the reason could be ascribed to the coordination effects of zero dimensional NCQDs, which can deposite on the surface of NCH nanosheets and prevent the growth of them [33]. Further structural detail is demonstrated in higher magnification image in Fig. 2 c, from which the ultrathin graphene-like nanosheet can be clearly observed. The heights of NCQDs, NCH and NCH/NCQDs-2 were characterized by AFM technique, and measured to be about 2.3, 6.5 and 5.7 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The lattice fringes of 0.24 and 0.21 nm correspond to the (031) and (100) crystal planes of NCH and NCQDs, respectively (Fig. 2d). In order to verify the distribution of each element in the NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets, TEM element mapping analysis was conducted. As presented in Fig. 2e-2i, the NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets mainly contain Ni, C, O and N elements, and each element is evenly distributed in the composites. The N element comes from NCQDs, which further confirms that the NCQDs have been successfully adhered to the surface of NCH.

XRD analysis was executed to investigate the crystalline structures of the NCH and NCH/NCQDs nanosheets. All the peaks in Fig. 3 a can be assigned to the monoclinic structure of Ni2(CO3)(OH)2 (JCPDS 35-0501). It is worth noting that no characteristic peaks of NCQDs can be found in the XRD pattern of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets due to the low crystallinity of CQDs [34]. The surface chemical states of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets were measured by XPS. As shown in Fig. S2, the NCH/NCQDs-2 composites mainly consist of Ni, C, O and N elements, which are consistent with the element mapping analysis. Fig. 3b presents the XPS spectrum of Ni 2p. The binding energy at 855.0 eV and its satellite (sat.) peak at 860.9 eV can be assigned to Ni 2p3/2, while the binding energy at 872.6 eV and its satellite peak at 878.5 eV correspond to Ni 2p1/2 [35,36]. In addition, the binding energy splitting (17.6 eV) represents the existence of Ni2+ in NCH/NCQDs-2 composites [37]. The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3c can be deconvoluted into three peaks, attributing to OH (530.6 eV), CO (531.1 eV) and CO (532.9 eV), respectively [19]. In the N 1s spectrum of NCH/NCQDs-2, the bands at 398.5, 399.6 and 400.5 eV can be classified as pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen, confirming that nitrogen atoms have been favourably doped into CQDs and the formed NCQDs have been coupled with NCH nanosheets. In order to analyze the specific surface area and porous structure of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm measurement was conducted. A representative type IV isotherm with H3 hysteresis loop can be found in Fig. 3e, which indicates the presence of mesopores in NCH/NCQDs-2 composites. The specific surface area of NCH/NCQDs-2 is determined to be 96.6 m2 g−1 and is much higher than the reported dandelion-like (Ni0.89Cu0.11)2(OH)2CO3 (18.1 m2 g−1) [38] and core–shell structured Cu(OH)2@Ni2(OH)2CO3 (69.5 m2 g−1) [15]. The pore size distribution determined by using the Barret-Joyner-Halenda technology (Fig. 3f) suggests that the main pore diameter is located at 3.8 nm. The enhanced specific surface area of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets with porous structure enable the electrolyte ions easily penetrate into the internal area of the electrode, and greatly improve the electrochemical properties [39,40].

The electrochemical characteristics of NCH and NCH/NCQDs nanosheets were firstly characterized in half-cell test configuration by cycle voltammetry (CV) tests [41]. Fig. 4 a depicts the CV profiles of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets at sweep rates from 0.2 to 1.0 mV s−1. A pair of distinct redox peaks correspond to the reversible conversion between Ni2+ and Ni3+. The faradaic redox reactions of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets can be depicted by the equations [42,43]:
(4) Ni2(CO3)(OH)2 + 4OH− ↔ 2NiOOH + CO3 2– + 2H2O + 2e−
(5) NiOOH + H2O + e− ↔ Ni(OH)2 + OH–

Because of the relatively slow kinetics of ion diffusion at higher sweep rates, the oxidation and reduction peaks appear positive and negative shifts with the increase of sweep rate [44]. The kinetics of NCH/NCQDs-2 electrode were calculated in accordance with the following formula [45,46]:
(6)
i = a
v
b
where v (mV s−1) and i (A g−1) represent the scan speed and peak current density in the CV profiles. The a and b are alterable parameters. The b value is obtained by the slope of log (i) versus log (v), and provides important information about the kinetics of charge storage. Generally, for b = 0.5, the current is dominated by battery-type behaviour controlled by diffusion, while b = 1.0 suggests a capacitive behavior [47]. In this study, as depicted in the lines in Fig. 4b, the b values are calculated to be about 0.59 and 0.52 for NCH/NCQDs-2 electrode. This indicates that the energy storage of NCH/NCQDs-2 is influenced by both the diffusion and capacitive process. The capacitive-controlled contribution of NCH/NCQDs-2 electrode can be determined quantitatively by the following equations [48,49]:
(7)
i
( V )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
(8)
i
( V )
/
v
1 / 2
=
k 1
v
1 / 2
+
k 2

In the above formulas, i(V) represents the current density, while k 1 v and k 2 v 1/2 are capacitive and diffusion-dominated contributions. The k 1 and k 2 are the alterable parameters. The k 1 value can be obtained by the slope of i(V)/v 1/2 versus v 1/2, and thus the capacitive contribution (k 1 v) can be evaluated. Herein, it is calculated that the ratios of capacitance contribution are 78.3%, 79.0%, 81.2%, 86.5% and 88.6% at sweep speeds of 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mV s−1. These values indicate that the ultrathin morphology of NCH/NCQDs-2 nanosheets is conducive to providing a large number of active sites on the surface and shortening the paths of ion transport [50]. The galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) curves of NCH/NCQDs-2 electrode at current densities from 1 to 15 A g−1 are presented in Fig. 4d. All the profiles are shown as quasi-symmetrical shapes, indicating the high coulomb efficiency and excellent reversibility of the electrode. According to equation (1), the specific capacities of NCH/NCQDs-2 are 727, 672.8, 597, 534 and 483 C g−1 at 1, 2, 5, 10 and 15 A g−1, respectively, which are comparable to previously reported NCH-based materials [12,51]. In order to compare the capacity performance of various electrode materials, GCD measurements of NCH with different NCQDs loadings were also performed (Fig. 4e and 4f). The specific capacities of NCH, NCH/NCQDs-1 and NCH/NCQDs-3 are 473, 657 and 701 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. In addition, the capacity retention of NCH/NCQDs-2 is found to maintain at 66.4% at 15 A g−1, which is superior to that of NCH (59.0%), NCH/NCQDs-1 (60.3%) and NCH/NCQDs-3 (65.1%) electrodes. Obviously, the NCH/NCQDs-2 possesses the highest capacity performance among all the electrodes. The enhancement of capacity performance of the NCH/NCQDs electrodes could be ascribed to the superb conductivity and facile surface functionalization of the CQDs. Although the NCQDs has a significant impact on the electrochemical properties of electrode materials. However, excessive NCQDs powder will hinder the contact of electrodes and electrolyte, resulting in a decrease in the specific capacity. Thus, the NCH/NCQDs-3 electrode presents relatively lower capacity performance. The cyclic stability of NCH/NCQDs-2 electrode was investigated by repeated GCD tests at 10 A g−1 (Fig. S3). After 5000 cycles, the capacity retention of NCH/NCQDs-2 is close to 82.4%, which is much larger than that of bare NCH (59.5%).

To further evaluate the practical applicability of NCH/NCQDs nanosheets, a hybrid supercapacitor device was fabricated with NCH/NCQDs-2 as cathode and PRGO as anode. Fig. 5 a displays the CV curves of NCH/NCQDs-2 and PRGO at 5 mV s−1. The NCH/NCQDs-2 and PRGO electrodes present reasonable electrochemical activity in the voltage window from −1.0 to 0.6 V. Thus, the voltage window of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO can be extended to 1.6 V. The representative CV graphs of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO are illustrated in Fig. 5b, from which a combination of battery-type faradaic behavior and capacitive charge storage can be observed. Moreover, as the scan rate increases from 5 to 50 mV s−1, the shape of CV curve is well remained without obvious distortion, which demonstrates the good reversibility of the NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO device. The corresponding specific capacitances of the device are calculated to be about 168.3, 151.7, 134.7, 121.3, 112.0 and 106.7 F g−1 at current densities from 1 to 20 A g−1 according to the discharge curves in Fig. 5c. Fig. 5e depicts the Ragone plots of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO device, which delivers a maximum energy density of 49.1 Wh kg−1 at 700.3 W kg−1, while 33.6 Wh kg−1 was retained when the power density increased to 15120 W kg−1. These values are comparable to the recently reported supercapacitor devices, such as Ni2(CO3)(OH)2//AC (32.7 Wh kg−1, 359.9 W kg−1) [51], NiCoCH31@GO//AC (45.8 Wh kg−1, 899 W kg−1) [52], NCC/PNTs//AC (41.86 Wh kg−1, 562 W kg−1) [53], Co(CO3)0.5(OH)/Ni2(CO3)(OH)2//AC (39.2 Wh kg−1, 800 W kg−1) [12], Ni0.75Co0.25(CO3)0.125(OH)2/C//rGH (44.4 Wh kg−1, 460 W kg−1) [54], Co2(CO3)(OH)2/NiCo(CO3)(OH)2//graphene (35.5 Wh kg−1, 2555.6 W kg−1) [55], RGO/Co(CO3)0.5(OH)·0.11H2O//RGO (42.9 Wh kg−1, 393 W kg−1) [56] and NiCo(CO3)(OH)2/NiMn(CO3)(OH)2//graphene (27.2 Wh kg−1, 702.7 W kg−1) [57]. The cyclic property of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO device at high current density of 10 A g−1 is displayed in Fig. 5f. After 8000 continuous GCD cycles, the capacitance retention of NCH/NCQDs-2//PRGO is close to 87.5%. Therefore, the decent electrochemical behaviors of NCH/NCQDs ultrathin nanosheets open up great application opportunities for further renewable energy.

In summary, NCQDs modified NCH composites were prepared by an effective hydrothermal strategy. Take advantage of this strategy, the obtained NCH/NCQDs composites display an ultrathin nanosheet architecture with an objective specific surface area. As supercapacitor electrode, the NCH/NCQDs nanosheets show a decent electrochemical capacity of 727 C g−1 at 1 A g−1 and enhanced cyclic stability of 82.4% retention after 5000 cycles at 10 A g−1, which are superior to the bare NCH. Moreover, the fabricated NCH/NCQDs//PRGO hybrid supercapacitor device delivers a maximum energy density of 49.1 Wh kg−1 at 700.3 W kg−1 and outstanding cycling performance for over 8000 cycles. Our study highlights the potentiality of the synthesized NCH/NCQDs ultrathin nanosheets for next generation supercapacitors.

Zhenyuan Ji: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Dongwei Ma: Data curation, Validation. Wenyao Dai: Data curation, Validation. Kai Liu: Visualization, Software. Xiaoping Shen: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Guoxing Zhu: Writing - review & editing. Yunjin Nie: Visualization, Validation. Drolma Pasang: Formal analysis, Software. Aihua Yuan: Formal analysis, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602129 and 21875091) and the Youth Talent Training Program of Jiangsu University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.01.102.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
